---
title: "On Hesse, Psychoanalysis and the Bildungsroman"
date: 2024-02-27
description: "Why Hermann Hesse's characters resonate, and what psychoanalysis has to do with it."
categories: [philosophy, literature]
draft: true
---

Hermann Hesse has long been among my favourite authors, for reasons not always clear to me. I know that I find his characters relatable — they are generally contemplative about the tensions and contradictions implicit in their psyches, which to me indicates a healthy sense of self-awareness that aligns with my values.

However, these characters are also drawn to — or troubled by — the seemingly weak veil of physical reality, the 'real world'. The realm of dreams, myth and mysticism play an important role in many of his characterisations.

So, the purpose of this foray is to come to a personal understanding. Why do I relate to Hesse's work so much?

## Characters

<!-- TODO -->

## Writing Style

His writing style suits the tension implicit in his themes, as he makes liberal use of exclamation points to highlight moments of emotion.

<!-- TODO: expand -->

## Psychoanalysis

Now, why are we talking about this at all? Well, it's because I don't know what to make of psychoanalysis. I am of course no expert in this domain, so further research may prove useful.

<!-- TODO: develop the connection between Hesse, Jungian psychology, and the Bildungsroman form -->
